speaker: interviewer
text: --a good sense of is what was going on with you just as you got liberated. I, I understand you were on a train. But I didn't get a good sense in the interview. You might--

speaker: interviewer
text: He went from Dachau to Buchenwald and then was on the train--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I got liberated when I-- when I got on the train and went back to Prague. In Prague is where I met my cousin Andy--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But he-- but he went more off of the idea-- I didn't get a good sense of it during the interview.

speaker: interviewer
text: You, you-- you're not getting a picture of the, the liberation. It happened-- he was on the train. There was an American soldier--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that they could see--

speaker: interviewer
text: Continuing with Andrew Meisels on the 15th of July 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: Andrew, can we back up a little bit to liberation on the train in the Munich vicinity? Can you just describe-- you mentioned the train going back and forth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all right. We didn't know that it was going back and forth. We just knew that the train is going. After so much time, it stopped. And then it started again. And then it stopped. And then it started again. We didn't know that some of it was going back on the same track that we just came from. And this was going on for, like, eight days and nights, or maybe seven nights and eight days, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know, at that time, that the end of the war was coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the scuttlebutt was that the Russians are getting close. It's imminent, that, that we're going to be liberated any time. Except the Germans are taking us now. Where the rumors started from, I don't know. Somebody always starts the rumor. The rumor was, they're, they're taking us to Tripoli. And they're going to kill us over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were hoping that maybe we were going to get a bomb. Talking about bombs, this happened many times as we were either walking or sitting inside the camp. And we saw hundreds of planes overhead. And we were yelling upstairs, give us a bomb, you know. We just wanted to die right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, it didn't happen. But this is what I'm talking about-- that we were in the train. And we were hoping that maybe something will happen before we get to Tripoli.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, sure enough, after-- three days before we were actually liberated, all the doors opened up. And the Swiss Red Cross gave everybody a package of, like, K-rations. So we knew something was up. Because why would they give us all these goodies? Why would they let the Red Cross stop and even talk to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't really talk to us. They just handed everybody, like, as if, this is yours, this is yours, this is yours, and so on. And then they closed it again. But we knew something was up. And sure enough, three days later, big silence. It just starting to get daylight when this happened that all of a sudden, I saw one American soldier with a Tommy gun and an Eisenhower jacket. I knew that this is it. And I said, this is an American. Well, and a few minutes later, all the doors opened up of these boxcars. And everybody rushed out. And everybody's rushing towards that one American. And within a few minutes, we're swarming with American soldiers. And that's when the major-- I think it was a major-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] announced that you are free now. And the jubilation is-- I cannot even describe to you, you know. And the first-- because we were making meals out of-- I'm talking about during the entire imprisonment-- meals out of anything you could. Some people found some grass. They seemed to think that this grass has some vitamins in there. Let's make a soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they boiled some water. And they put some salt in there. And it was a soup. It was probably plain grass. Or maybe there was four-leaf-- a few four-leafs clovers.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you put something in you. And that's what happened during the time when I was digging ditches. I found a piece of meat. It was part of a leg of a horse. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the leg, not the whole horse?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not the whole horse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that from bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how it got there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just-- I just cut off a piece, put it in my pocket-- I must have smelled-- put it in my pocket, and, and re-buried it. And then when I got back to camp, I-- with my two other friends, like the brother-in-law of my cousin and the guy that was marching with me, when I told him, what am I going to tell your mother? Then somehow or other, we made-- we made a fire inside the-- half, half-baked or half-cooked, we ate it, you know. And it smelled. But it was food. So-- and we did that for quite a few days like that, off of that same leg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get sick from it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't get sick from it. But I'm sick now. [LAUGHTER] My stomach is not what it used to be, not what the average person has. I cannot absorb the food as easily. And neither can Andy, my cousin. He's got all kinds of problems, also. And probably that has a lot to do-- I ate-- I ate Angora rabbit, for example. I never ate rabbit in my life, but Angora rabbit, nobody eats. Somebody [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came back with two or three rabbits like that. This is already a day after we got liberated, one day after we got liberated. And then I realized, you get-- you get different kinds of food in the kitchen. It was open 24 hours. And we were-- and I was eating, building myself up. Two weeks after that, or maybe it was three, I went to the major. And I said, I'd like to make my way home. And he said, feel free to do it. There are trucks leaving here every day to Pilsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pilsen was Czechoslovakia, all right? That part was liberated by Eisenhower, which he later turned over to the Russians-- Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --like handing you a piece of land or, or an entire country on a-- on a silver platter. And the Russians took it over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that's where I met my cousin Andy-- in Prague. And from that point on, we traveled together until we got to my cousin in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Andrew, unless you have something else you want to add or tell-- share with us, I think we'll pause now and, and meet your wife and your cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that I'd rather give the time so you can meet my cousin and my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: --red light.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'd like you to meet my wife, Judy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewer
text: Judy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my cousin, also Andy-- Andy Meisels-- same first name, same last--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: --name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there's still a third one, by the way-- Andy Meisels.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: But he's, he's a reporter for Associated Press--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Israel. He's stationed in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Andy, I hope we'll have the honor of interviewing you in the near future.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I think it's impossible for us to get a, a good story in a few minutes. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. I'm sure-- I have a different story, quite different from his. And hopefully, it will be just as meaningful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Judy, could I ask you a couple questions about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you first became aware of the fact that Andrew was a survivor? Was it from the beginning of your courtship, did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did--

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me briefly, when we first met. And, of course, I saw the, the tattoo on his arm, the numbers on his arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of impression did it make on you? Were you afraid of it? Were you in awe of it? What were your first impressions when, when he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gee, you're talking over 30 years ago. I, I was curious. I wanted to know more about it. I was very young during the war and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] didn't really know too much about it except what I had read. But here was someone that could tell me firsthand what happened and what he went through. And so that was interesting for me. And I was shocked when he told me some of the things. But I always wanted to hear more. And at some point, he didn't want to say any more for that particular time. It was too emotional for him. And so, little by little, he told me things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there times when you thought life was more difficult because of his past experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure of it. I'm sure of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the nightmares he used to have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- he still has them occasionally, yes. In fact, more than just occasionally-- there are many times when he's moaning in his sleep. And I have to nudge him to wake him up. And he said-- he tells me-- he's told me that he had a bad dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think the children have been affected by his experiences at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Affected in what way? I'm not too sure what you mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not sure, either, because we don't know really in what way. Just-- we're sort of still asking the questions whether--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my son especially. My daughter also, but I-- my son is very sensitive to what has happened in the past. He's very interested in the Holocaust. And even when he was little, he used to ask his father about some of the things that happened. And of course, Andy wouldn't go into great detail because Gary was little at the time. But Gary was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he couldn't understand how Andy had brothers that-- had brothers, younger brothers and an older brother, and they were not alive. And Andy explained to him what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know one time, Gary was very little. And he asked if they were up in heaven. And Andy said, yes. And how will-- how will they know when Andy comes? How will they know where he is? How will they know where to meet him? And Andy said, they'll meet me. And, and Gary also said well, what happens when I go up to heaven? And Andy said, I'll wait for you at the gate. Little things that-- Andy mentioned about his, his, home life and things that he did with his brothers and his family. And Gary was very interested in it. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, Judy is 11 years younger than I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 and 1/2. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Andy, I, I know your relationship. I mean, I know you're cousins. But can you tell me, it was your-- it was your, your mother whose brother-- what's the relationship--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my, my mother and his father were brother and sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: That it is. His, his, his mother was my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: His father was my uncle through marriage. But also, he was my father's first cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this-- in the same place he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. And where were you during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- during the war, I was in Munkacs until deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was deported to Auschwitz along with everyone else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And just briefly, I was in Auschwitz. And I spent two months in the Warsaw Ghetto. But that was not in the-- it was a concentration camp built on the Warsaw Ghetto. And, as a matter of fact, the anniversary is coming up August the 1st-- there was an uprising in the city. And that's how I was temporarily liberated and ultimately taken to Germany as, as a-- as a Gentile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the rest of the war, I lived under an assumed name.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: --as, as a-- so we have a real person here on camera. There is a very important story in what you're telling us. So I hope you will give us an opportunity--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, be glad to.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to, to interview you. And if I could just ask you, Andrew, you came from a religious home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you and Judy keep a religious home now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house, we keep kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: You do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And observe all the holidays and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, yes. I'm not strictly Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your faith was never shaky after the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was shaky to the degree that I am not as strict as my father would have liked me-- to see me, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do turn on the light on Saturday, for example. And in Europe, we were not allowed to do that. It's just-- I, I-- to, to tell it to you in short, that I'm not strict at all, you know, with the religion. But I know exactly where we come from, and where-- how I was brought up. I keep the tradition more than the strict Orthodox religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much. I want to thank all three of you for being here. And Andrew, thank you very much for allowing us to do this interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Judy.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a cut. And you're on.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, what do I tell you? Just Just tell me who's in-- who's in the picture. In the picture is my, my dad, my mom, my sister, Margaret, who lives in Montreal. That was me. That's my youngest-- no, the younger brother, one below me, Ernest. That's my older brother, David. And that's my youngest brother. He was seven years old. This picture was taken, like, in 1937, '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, great. I'm just going to make sure I get a really nice shot of the picture. I should have paused, but I didn't. I will. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my son, Gary. He was 30 years old last January. And that's the love of my life. And then I'll introduce you to my daughter, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter Karen. She was 28 in January-- last January. And she's in the film industry. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, what does she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she's a--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family, taken a few years earlier, as you can see from their hairdo-- from Gary and Karen's hairdo, which is out by now, thank goodness. And this is-- was Judy's mother. She passed away last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, it just a was a matter of me framing it up. I just didn't frame it up right. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And do I do it again?

speaker: interviewer
text: Nah, I think--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --it'll be all right. This is another shot of Andrew and Judy.

speaker: interviewee
text: --she left a lot of them last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: --it's crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he a survivor also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because Gary's alone--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was from Budapest. He lives in Israel--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.
